title: When Classical ML Plateaus
concept: Classical ML shines on small and medium structured datasets, especially clean tables with meaningful columns, but once performance stops improving, extra manual feature tweaking often produces smaller and smaller gains.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the big promise of deep learning compared with classical ML?

options.text: It can learn many useful features from raw or lightly processed data
It works only when there are no labels
It makes every small spreadsheet problem harder on purpose

exercises.explain: Right. But that does not erase classical ML. On the right kind of data, classical models are still amazingly strong.

cards.title: A beautiful spreadsheet is a classical ML playground
cards.body: If your data already comes as clean columns — age, income, attendance, clicks, temperature, balance — classical ML often feels right at home. The important clues are already laid out for you, which means the feature-learning burden is much smaller.

cards.title: What does "plateau" mean here?
cards.body: A plateau happens when you keep cleaning data, tuning models, and inventing extra features, but each new idea buys only a tiny improvement. The model is still useful — you are just getting diminishing returns from more manual tweaking.

cards.title: Squeezing the last drops
cards.body: Imagine squeezing a lemon. The first squeeze gives a lot of juice. Later squeezes take more effort for only a few extra drops. Classical ML on tabular data often feels like that once you have already used the most obvious features.

cards.title: A school dashboard example
cards.body: Suppose a school wants to predict which students may need extra support using a table of attendance, homework completion, quiz averages, and tardies. A tree model or gradient boosting model can do very well fast. But after that, adding one more custom ratio or hand-made score may only nudge the metric a tiny bit.

cards.title: Plateau does not mean failure
cards.body: Hitting a plateau does not mean classical ML is bad or outdated. In fact, on small and medium tabular datasets, it is often still the best first choice because it trains faster, needs less data, and is easier to explain.

cards.title: Classical ML's sweet spot
cards.body: Where classical models usually feel strongest:

left.text:
Clean spreadsheet columns
Small or medium dataset
Strong performance quickly

right.text:
More manual feature tricks
More tuning effort
Tinier gains each round

exercises.prompt: Which project is classical ML often strongest on right away?

options.text: Predicting house prices from a clean table of 20 meaningful columns and 15,000 rows
Learning directly from millions of raw satellite photos
Generating music from raw sound data

exercises.explain: Exactly. Clean, structured tabular data is classical ML territory.

exercises.prompt: True or false: On clean tabular data, deep learning automatically beats classical ML no matter the dataset size.
exercises.explain: False. Classical models are often extremely competitive — and sometimes better — on structured tables, especially when data is not enormous.

exercises.sentence: On structured spreadsheet data, classical ML often starts strong because the useful ___ are already laid out in columns.
exercises.explain: Exactly. When the features are already meaningful and organized, classical ML gets a head start.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best shows a classical ML plateau?

options.text: A team keeps adding tiny hand-made ratios to the same churn table and each change barely improves accuracy
A student labels a dataset for the first time
A spreadsheet is saved as a CSV file

exercises.explain: Right. The model is still working — the point is that more manual feature engineering is giving smaller and smaller returns.

exercises.prompt: A school has an attendance table with 8,000 rows and meaningful columns. The team already tried linear models, trees, boosting, and several hand-made features, but the gains are now tiny. What does this most likely mean?

options.text: The project is hitting a classical ML plateau on this structured data
It proves the data has no pattern at all
Adding random extra columns will guarantee a huge jump
Deep learning is always required from day one

exercises.explain: Exactly. Classical ML often gets strong results quickly on tables, then levels off. That is one reason researchers got excited about a different approach for harder, messier inputs.
